era_context: The Roman Empire under Hadrian (r. 117–138 CE) is at its territorial zenith. The Bar Kokhba Revolt erupts in Judaea, marking a major Jewish-Roman conflict. The Eastern Han dynasty in China is experiencing increasing court instability. The Kushan Empire dominates Central Asia and the Silk Road trade routes.

events.title: Bar Kokhba Revolt begins in Judaea
events.description: The Bar Kokhba Revolt erupts in 132 CE, a major Jewish uprising against Roman rule in Judaea. Led by Simon bar Kokhba (also known as Simon bar Kosiba), who is hailed by some as the Messiah, the rebellion is triggered by Hadrian's decision to rebuild Jerusalem as a pagan city (Aelia Capitolina) and to erect a temple to Jupiter Capitolinus on the site of the Jewish Temple. The revolt begins with guerrilla warfare and quickly escalates into a full-scale military conflict. Jewish forces initially achieve significant successes, controlling much of Judaea and minting their own coins. The rebellion will last until 135 CE, when it is finally suppressed by Roman forces.
key_figures: Sextus Julius Severus (Roman general, arrives c. 133 CE)

sources.name: Bar Kokhba letters and documents from Judean Desert caves
Numismatic evidence (coins minted by Bar Kokhba rebels)

events.certainty_note: The Bar Kokhba Revolt's beginning in 132 CE is well-attested in multiple sources, including contemporary letters and coins. The dating is secure.
cross_references: Follows from the First Jewish-Roman War (66–70 CE)
Leads to the suppression of the revolt in 135 CE
Marks a turning point in Jewish-Roman relations

events.title: Hadrian's decision to rebuild Jerusalem as Aelia Capitolina
events.description: Hadrian issues a decree to rebuild Jerusalem as a pagan city, to be renamed Aelia Capitolina (Aelia from his family name Aelius, Capitolina from Jupiter Capitolinus). A temple to Jupiter Capitolinus is to be erected on the site of the Jewish Temple. This decision is a major factor in triggering the Bar Kokhba Revolt. The rebuilding reflects Hadrian's policy of Hellenization and his desire to integrate Judaea more fully into the Roman Empire. However, the policy proves deeply offensive to the Jewish population and contributes to the outbreak of the rebellion.
key_figures: Jewish leaders and rabbis opposed to the policy
events.certainty_note: Hadrian's decision to rebuild Jerusalem is well-attested in multiple sources. The dating to 132 CE (or possibly slightly earlier) is secure.
cross_references: Directly triggers the Bar Kokhba Revolt (132-001)
Part of Hadrian's broader policy of Hellenization and urbanization

events.title: Eastern Han court: Emperor Shun's reign begins
events.description: Emperor Shun (r. 125–144 CE) is a child emperor who ascends to the throne following the death of his predecessor. His reign is characterized by regency rule and increasing eunuch influence. By 132 CE, the pattern of child emperors and powerful regents is well-established in the Eastern Han court. The imperial succession is unstable, and factional struggles between eunuchs, imperial consorts' families, and scholar-officials are frequent. This period marks the beginning of the institutional decline that will eventually lead to the dynasty's collapse.

sources.name: Hou Hanshu (History of the Later Han), compiled 5th century CE

events.certainty_note: Emperor Shun's reign dates are well-established in Chinese historical records. The year 132 CE falls within his reign, which began in 125 CE.
cross_references: Part of the decline of the Eastern Han dynasty
Contemporary with Roman political stability under Hadrian (contrast)

events.title: Kushan Empire: continued expansion and cultural patronage
events.description: The Kushan Empire, under Kanishka I (r. c. 127–150 CE) or his successors, continues to dominate Central Asia and the Silk Road trade routes. The Kushans are major patrons of Buddhism, and Buddhist monasteries and stupas are constructed throughout their territory. The empire's multicultural character is reflected in its coins, which bear inscriptions in Greek, Persian, and Indian languages. By 132 CE, Kushan power is at or near its zenith, and the empire serves as a crucial conduit for the transmission of Buddhism along the Silk Road.
key_figures: Kanishka I (Kushan emperor, r. c. 127–150 CE)
Buddhist monks and scholars patronized by the Kushan court

sources.name: Chinese historical records (Hou Hanshu, compiled later)

events.certainty_note: The Kushan Empire's dominance of Central Asia in the 2nd century CE is well-established. However, the exact dating of Kanishka's reign is debated. The year 132 CE is assigned by convention as a marker within this period of Kushan prominence.
cross_references: Contemporary with Roman-Parthian tensions over trade routes
Facilitates the spread of Buddhism to China and beyond
Part of the broader Silk Road trade network

disconfirming_evidence: The exact trigger for the Bar Kokhba Revolt is debated. While Hadrian's decision to rebuild Jerusalem as Aelia Capitolina is traditionally cited as the primary cause, some scholars argue that the revolt may have been triggered by other factors, such as economic grievances or religious tensions. The dating of Kanishka's reign is one of the most contentious issues in Indian history, with scholars proposing dates ranging from c. 78 CE to c. 150 CE. This uncertainty affects the precise chronology of Kushan-Roman and Kushan-Chinese interactions.
historiographic_note: The year 132 CE is well-documented for the Roman Empire and the Bar Kokhba Revolt, with multiple contemporary and near-contemporary sources available. However, the record is heavily weighted toward military and political events. For the Kushan Empire, numismatic and archaeological evidence is rich, but written sources are limited. For China, the historical record is rich but filtered through later compilations. The experiences of ordinary people, women, and non-elite populations are largely absent. Sub-Saharan Africa, Northern Europe, and the Americas have no synchronized written records for this year.

graph_edges.note: Second major Jewish-Roman conflict, following the First Jewish-Roman War (66–70 CE)
The Bar Kokhba Revolt begun in 132 CE is suppressed in 135 CE
Hadrian's decision to rebuild Jerusalem directly triggers the Bar Kokhba Revolt
The institutional decline of the Eastern Han under Emperor Shun contributes to the dynasty's collapse in 220 CE
